title: Why do bug fixes lead to constant response calls (CRs)? A personal reflection on the SpecKit architecture design.
description: A record of encountering consecutive CRs while fixing bugs in SpecKit (formerly Codex CLI): reviewing implicit coupling in the system and discussing personal views on the ideal architecture design for future evolution.
content:
> **TL;DR**: 在修复 SpecKit（原 Codex CLI）命令调用失败的 Bug 时，我遭遇了 Copilot 连续不断的 CR。复盘发现，这并非单纯的代码错误，而是系统缺乏“单一真源”导致的架构隐式耦合。虽然当前 PR 仅在于填平不一致的坑点，但以此为契机，我也推演了未来应当演进的“统一领域核心”架构设计，并分享一些个人设计理念看法。 I recently spent a significant amount of time dealing with a seemingly minor issue: on SpecKit (formerly Codex CLI), users couldn't smoothly invoke commands and trigger a fixed, streamlined workflow like with other agents. Initially, my assessment of the problem was quite simplistic: it should be a localized fix. A few tweaks to naming conventions, generation logic, and invocation methods, some additional testing, and that would be it. However, it turned out to be completely different. During the fix process, Copilot Reviews kept providing new CRs. One was fixed, another popped up; a seemingly local change eventually unraveled a whole chain of issues involving presets, extensions, hooks, release packaging, and Kimi compatibility migrations. The pressure was immense, and it was incredibly energy-consuming. Finally, the problem was resolved, and the number of PRs decreased. But the most valuable aspect of this period wasn't actually "fixing the bug," but rather using the process to clearly see some real architectural problems within the codebase. This article aims to share the retrospective analysis behind this fix. ---## 起点：一个看起来很小的问题 The initial problem was specific: The experience of invoking quick workflows via commands in the SpecKit terminal was incorrect. Users expected to quickly enter a standardized workflow in a skill-like manner; however, the current implementation's naming, generation, and invocation methods were not fully aligned, leading to inconsistent experiences and, in some cases, failing to correctly target the actual output. From a purely functional perspective, this seemed like a minor tweak to the naming or generation logic. But digging deeper revealed that the problem wasn't limited to a single feature. Because this capability is far more than just "a file generator." It's connected to: - agent 配置 - command 到 skill 的映射 - preset 安装和卸载 - extension 自动注册 - hook 的调用提示 - GitHub release 的打包脚本 - 本地 `uv run specify`Differences from remote release packages- Kimi 的历史兼容和迁移逻辑 In other words, you think you're fixing a button, but you're actually touching a distributed protocol system. ---## 为什么 Copilot 会不断 CR The most stressful part of this process wasn't the difficulty of writing the code, but the constant stream of new review requests (CRs) from Copilot. Initially, a natural reaction was: Had I missed too many things? Was the review bot "smarter" than me? Later, my assessment changed: It wasn't that it was smarter, but rather that it considered the consistency of the entire PR, while I was initially focusing on fixing issues within the current thread. These two perspectives are vastly different. Much of what I did at the time essentially meant: - 修一个已有评论指出的问题 - 让当前测试通过 - 确保行为在这个局部成立 But Copilot is looking at something else entirely: - 这个改动和别的模块约定一致吗 - 生成和消费是对称的吗 - 命名规则是不是在所有入口都统一了 - 删除、恢复、迁移是不是完整闭环 - 本地逻辑和 release 产物是不是一致 This is precisely why many problems manifest themselves in this way: - 代码本身看起来没错 - 测试也能通过 - 但放到整条链路里，就是会漂 What I truly felt this time was: **Many CRs are not "implementation errors", but rather "system invariants are not expressed uniformly".** --- ## 压力的根源，不只是 CR 多 To be honest, this round of review was very stressful. Because it wasn't the kind of pressure where you "made a mistake in an if statement" and then it was over; rather: - 你修掉一个问题 - review 再从另一个入口指出另一个不一致 - 你会开始怀疑自己对全局的理解是不是不够完整 - 每次 push 都可能再引出新 thread This feeling is very different from simply fixing bugs. It's more like patching holes in the dark within a system without a clear central focus. Looking back, what truly put pressure on me wasn't the number of comments themselves, but rather something it revealed: **I initially underestimated the discreteness of this business logic.**I thought I was fixing a feature, but I was actually dealing with a network of rules scattered across multiple modules, scripts, and artifact chains. ---## 用一张图看清当前的 skill 架构 The most striking point for me this time is that the problem isn't in a single function, but rather that the entire skill chain's "production-consumption-recovery-migration" process is quite fragmented. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Producers["Skill 产物生产端"] InitPy["Python: install_ai_skills()"] CmdReg["Python: CommandRegistrar.render_skill_command()"] ExtReg["Python: ExtensionManager._register_extension_skills()"] PresetReg["Python: PresetManager._register_skills()"] ReleaseSH["Bash: create-release-packages.sh"] ReleasePS["PowerShell: create-release-packages.ps1"] end subgraph Artifact["核心产物"] SkillFile["//SKILL.md"] end subgraph Consumers["Skill 产物消费端"] Hook["Hook invocation / hook message"] ExtRemove["Extension remove / cleanup"] PresetRemove["Preset remove / restore"] Migration["Legacy migration"] end InitPy --> SkillFile CmdReg --> SkillFile ExtReg --> SkillFile PresetReg --> SkillFile ReleaseSH --> SkillFile ReleasePS --> SkillFile SkillFile --> Hook SkillFile --> ExtRemove SkillFile --> PresetRemove SkillFile --> Migration ```The most important aspect of this chart isn't the number of lines, but rather the fact it illustrates: **The same`SKILL.md`There are many producers and many consumers.**This means: - 只改一个生成逻辑，不代表别的生成逻辑也跟着改了 - 只改一个消费逻辑，不代表别的恢复/删除逻辑也理解同样的规则 - 本地 Python 修好了，不代表 GitHub release 包也已经正确 - Kimi / Codex / extension / preset / hook 之间，任何一处没同步，都会造成漂移 This is why CRs (Content Requests) kept popping up in this PR. You think you're fixing a single point, but you're actually touching a whole network. ---## 为什么一个小改动会引发一串 CR If we were to abstract this problem into a single sentence, it would be: **One rule is that there is no single source of truth.**For example, the "skill naming convention" is not a unified interface in the current architecture; instead, it's scattered across many places. ```mermaid flowchart LR Change["修改一条 skill 规则
例如：speckit.plan -> speckit-plan"] A["__init__.py
init / migration"] B["agents.py
command -> skill 渲染"] C["extensions.py
extension 注册 / hook"] D["presets.py
preset 覆盖 / restore"] E["release .sh
远程包生成"] F["release .ps1
远程包生成"] Change --> A Change --> B Change --> C Change --> D Change --> E Change --> F ```The intuitive feeling this structure evokes is: - 改动面比看起来大很多 - 很容易漏改 - 漏掉一处，系统表面还能运行，但 review 很快会把它揪出来 - 测试如果不是按“不变量”写，也很难一次性发现 Copilot's repeated submissions to the CR (Conformity Assessment) are not due to nitpicking, but rather because this type of architecture inherently exposes inconsistencies. ---## 这次暴露出来的几个核心问题 ### 1. skill 不是一等对象，而是一堆字符串约定 The most obvious problem this time is that the concept of "skill" wasn't modeled as a stable domain object. It's more like a set of conventions scattered throughout: - 目录名怎么命名 - frontmatter 里的 `name`How to write- hook 提示时怎么渲染 invocation - preset remove 时怎么恢复 - extension install 时怎么生成 - release 脚本里怎么打包 Many of these rules are not expressed through a unified interface, but rather implemented separately through string concatenation, path conventions, and YAML assembly. The result is: Changing the naming conventions for skills will almost certainly affect the entire skill chain. ### 2. 生产端和消费端不是单一来源 I went back and checked it again later to fully understand the problem: - Python 代码本地会生成 skill - shell / PowerShell 的 release 脚本也会生成 skill - 默认 `specify init`The priority is still to use the GitHub release package. What does this mean? It means that even if your local code is correct, it doesn't mean the user "gets the correct version by default." This is a particularly typical pitfall this time: Local`--offline`The generated content is correct, but the Kimi skill metadata in the remote release package is still outdated, resulting in inconsistencies in the user's default initialization. This kind of problem is very difficult to spot intuitively unless the system has a single, genuine source. ### 3. 生命周期逻辑是分散的 If a system only handles "generation," many problems wouldn't be so obvious. But skills aren't just generated once; they involve a complete lifecycle: - init 时生成 - extension 安装时注册 - preset 安装时覆盖 - hook 消费时提示调用 - remove 时恢复或删除 - 兼容历史命名时迁移 These actions are scattered across different documents, coordinating with each other through implicit conventions. Without a unified model, this problem easily arises: - create 的逻辑改了 - remove 没跟着改 - restore 仍然按旧规则理解 - hook 还在提示旧名字 This bug is very persistent because each function seems "logical" on its own, but the system concatenates them incorrectly. ### 4. migration 只是“挪目录”，不是“语义迁移” A very typical example of this is the migration of Kimi's historical skills. The directory names have been moved from the old format to the new format, but...`SKILL.md`Inside`frontmatter.name`It wasn't changed. This resulted in a very awkward situation: - 目录是新的 - 元数据还是旧的 These kinds of issues indicate that migration only performs file system-level moves, without addressing artifact semantic normalization. This might be a workable compromise in the early stages, but it becomes a long-term liability as the system grows more complex. ---## 这次修复最终解决了什么 Although the process was quite troublesome, the main problems were ultimately resolved. Including: - Codex 和 Kimi 的 skill naming 对齐 - hook invocation 和真实 skill 目录对齐 - extension 自动注册 skill 的 placeholder/path 解析补齐 - preset remove 时的 restore / cleanup 对称性修复 - Kimi 在 native skill 模式下和 `ai_skills`Switch semantic alignment- 一批围绕 malformed config、非 dict init-options、缺失 `SKILL.md`Boundary behavior reinforcement From the results, these issues are not "single-point bug fixes," but rather a gradual process of bringing the entire skill chain back to a consistent state. ---## 更重要的收获：以后不能只按 thread 修 After this experience, I have a very clear idea: **In the future, when handling such PRs, we can no longer simply passively modify them according to the review thread.**A full local audit must be conducted first, answering the following questions: - 同一个概念是不是有多套命名规则 - create / restore / unregister / remove 是否对称 - hook 渲染和真实产物是否一致 - 本地生成逻辑和 release 产物是否一致 - Python、bash、PowerShell 是否各自维护了一套业务规则 - 测试是在验证新行为，还是还在锁旧行为 If we don't review things this way first, and instead directly modify based on existing comments, it's easy to fall into a state of local optima while the overall picture continues to drift. ---## 探讨：如果做一次架构整改，目标应该是什么 Given the numerous implicit couplings in the current system, if it's to converge in a more reasonable direction in the future, I believe the ideal form of the skill logic (in my personal opinion) should look like this: ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph App["应用层 / 用例层"] InitSvc["InitService"] ExtSvc["ExtensionService"] PresetSvc["PresetService"] HookSvc["HookService"] ReleaseSvc["ReleaseBuildService"] end subgraph Domain["统一领域核心"] SkillSpec["SkillArtifact / SkillSpec"] Naming["SkillNamingPolicy"] Render["SkillRenderer"] Parse["SkillParser"] Validate["SkillInvariantValidator"] Registry["SkillRegistry / Ownership Model"] end subgraph Adapters["适配层"] FS["Filesystem Adapter"] GH["GitHub Release Builder"] Shell["Bash / PowerShell Wrapper"] end App --> Domain InitSvc --> SkillSpec ExtSvc --> SkillSpec PresetSvc --> SkillSpec HookSvc --> Naming ReleaseSvc --> SkillSpec SkillSpec --> Naming SkillSpec --> Render SkillSpec --> Parse SkillSpec --> Validate SkillSpec --> Registry Render --> FS ReleaseSvc --> GH Shell --> ReleaseSvc FS --> SkillFile["//SKILL.md"] GH --> ReleaseAsset["release asset / template zip"] ```The core idea behind this image can be summed up in one sentence: **A skill should first be a domain object, and then a file.**In other words: - 命名规则归 `SkillNamingPolicy`- frontmatter/body 渲染归 `SkillRenderer`- 解析和校验归 `SkillParser` / `SkillInvariantValidator`- ownership 和 source-of-truth 归 `Registry`- shell / PowerShell 不再自己实现业务规则，只做 adapter After doing this,`SKILL.md`It is no longer the control plane itself, but merely a disk-based result of domain objects. ---## 假想的改造对比：修改一条规则时的感受差别 ### 当前状态 ```mermaid flowchart LR Rule["修改 skill 命名 / frontmatter / path 规则"] Rule --> A["init"] Rule --> B["preset"] Rule --> C["extension"] Rule --> D["hook"] Rule --> E["bash release"] Rule --> F["powershell release"] ```The immediate impression is: **Changing a rule is like defusing six bombs at the same time.** ### 理想中的整改后状态 ```mermaid flowchart LR Rule["修改 skill 命名 / frontmatter / path 规则"] Rule --> Core["Skill Domain Core"] Core --> A["init"] Core --> B["preset"] Core --> C["extension"] Core --> D["hook"] Core --> E["release builder"] ```The intuitive feeling will become: **To change a rule, first modify the central part, then allow the outer parts to reuse it.**These two feelings are vastly different. The former is about "potential leaks everywhere"; the latter is the true goal—"the default assumption that things won't drift." ---## 为什么这对 extension 模式特别重要 After this review, I've made a strong judgment: SpecKit is no longer just a scaffolding CLI; it's evolving into a platform with an extension ecosystem. And once platformized, the extension model will amplify the following problems: - skill naming 是否全局统一 - release 产物和本地逻辑是否一致 - hook invocation 是否稳定命中真实产物 - preset / extension 的覆盖和恢复是否对称 - catalog / priority / ownership 是否可解释、可审计、可回滚 If these rules continue to be scattered across: - Python 模块 - Bash 脚本 - PowerShell 脚本 - 文件系统约定 - frontmatter 元数据 Therefore, with each additional agent and each new extension, the complexity continues to increase. Conversely, if this domain layer is consolidated first, subsequent expansion capabilities will be healthier. ---## 这次最真实的感受 If I had to summarize this experience in a more subjective way, I would say this: **Under the current architecture, modifying skill-related code is like repairing a road on an old map;** **Only when the aforementioned domain modeling overhaul is completed will it truly resemble adding rules to a real transportation system.**The former relies on experience, patches, and caution; the latter, in my opinion, is the correct model that can truly support the healthy development of the extension ecosystem. ---## 写在最后 This restoration experience made me realize something quite directly: **Many stressful reviews are not because you can't fix bugs, but because the system itself hasn't properly organized its rules.**When business logic is sufficiently discrete, any change naturally amplifies its impact. At this point, reviews are no longer just about "finding bugs," but rather reminding you: there are too many implicit couplings in this system. From the outcome, the issues were eventually resolved. But for me, the more valuable aspect was that this debriefing process revealed what truly needed fixing—not just the immediate bugs, but also the expressive capabilities required for the next stage of architectural design. If this personal discussion on "ideal architecture" can offer some reference to others maintaining complex CLIs, plugin systems, and workflow systems, then this effort has been worthwhile. ---## 推荐阅读 If you are interested in SpecKit or system refactoring evolution, we recommend reading other related articles on this site:- [SpecKit 结构化文档驱动（SDD）设计与实践](/posts/sdd-series-part-2-spec-kit/) - [复杂 CLI 的演进之路：基于前端重构底层基础设施](/posts/rust-core-npm-shell-cli-evolution/)
